sentences.text: You want me to finish with--
Goth.
Goth.
So all the bodies were laying over there on the soil, ground.
And there was a big sign up above their heads that they wanted-- there was a-- how you call this?
They tried to run away from ghetto, and they got a lot of guns, and that's the reason he shot them.
Well, this [INAUDIBLE] too, because there was one German, [INAUDIBLE], which-- [INAUDIBLE], I mean, she was in the ladies department watching the girl, and he used to beat up the girls' face.
No, German.
German.
And she went on vacation to Germany, naturally stopped in Berlin and [INAUDIBLE].
And they ask her, hey, what happened over there?
I hear you got all things, story, in the new concentration camp.
And he says, what story?
[INAUDIBLE].
There was guns and this.
And I says, where did you hear this?
Then she hesitated, and that's how it come out.
What happened?
They threw over them, because, you know, with Gestapo over there.
So she should told them the truth, who was Goth?
He got this [INAUDIBLE] thing.
And he didn't.
There was no emergency over there or danger to him.
So they start investigating Goth, and finish him off.
I mean, they changed him as SS commander with another one.
Who was the next one who came after Goth?
[INAUDIBLE] I think his name was.
A nice guy, because don't forget one thing.
We didn't have information, but always something.
The war ends.
I mean, the Americans-- they're already near the Rhine in Germany, and the Russian, a near thing.
So the question what happen to us?
Are they going to kill us before?
How we'll be surviving?
Oh, this was when we-- Chilowitch was with the liquidated camp where my brother was shot.
They sent a few people, including Chilowitch, who had became our there a big shot with the Gestapo, you know.
They send him to the other [INAUDIBLE], town, him and his wife.
And when they start cleaning the [INAUDIBLE], you know, so they surround the town.
They took the old people outside the cemetery, shot them.
And then organized Polish peasants with horse and buggies to bring the people, the rest, to a railroad station in [INAUDIBLE].
I was on one of the-- I think my wife and another two people.
My brother-in-law, my wife's brother, was not [INAUDIBLE], and she was on the same-- Chilowitch's wife on the same horse and buggy with me.
I tried to talk to-- everything was a Polish policeman marching.
I tried to talk him.
Listen, let me run away.
I'll give you some money.
I got my things underneath, take it.
No, he says.
Not only I wouldn't take it.
I'm going to punch you.
Why you want to run away?
And you know what's going to happen to you.
So I felt one thing.
What have you got to lose?
We were going downhill, and she told to me, Henry, let's run away.
It's no good.
Downhill on a curve, I says, listen, to my wife.
You run first from the thing, and I ran after you.
And she was the third one.
What I did, I took a bunch of money from my pocket, threw it in this policeman's face.
So he didn't know what to do-- count the money or watch me, and we jumped from the thing.
All the parade passed.
I find my wife, and I saw Chilowitch's wife and my brother-in-law.
So I was jumping.
He jumped, too.
So all through the night we were going to a railroad station and tried to get back to Krakow.
And actually I look, went to buy tickets.
There was no problem.
Only later this ticket-- who sold the ticket saw my brother-in-law.
Hey, who is he?
I figure it's something, so I says, listen.
He want to run away.
I said, I don't now who is he.
He run away.
Somehow he survived, too.
We came to Krakow over all night rails.
Took a railroad train.
Somehow we came up, and in the ghetto, I told you before what happened when I came.
And she went to her husband and says, listen, thanks to him I'm alive.
So I got little always favors from him.
He wasn't interested.
He wasn't.
No, he was nothing when I came to Krakow.
He came to Krakow to visit his wife because she was with her parents.
He came from Mielec.
And the one day I remember we were staying in [INAUDIBLE], and he shows me, look who's coming through the door.
It was chief of Gestapo from Mielec.
Right away he run to him.
He knew him from Mielec.
And the deal was he went to the Jewish police and recommended him.
And they should take him to the police.
He's a good man.
I remember I got a relative who was connected with chief of police, Mr.
[INAUDIBLE].
And I told him, listen, you want trouble from this guy?
Take him to police, [INAUDIBLE], because he's a trouble maker.
He's going, take [INAUDIBLE] job in 24 hours.
So what they did, they send him [INAUDIBLE] to Krakow-Plaszow.
And today she has sent people over there.
They send him to over there, and they made him chief.
This is Chilowitch?
Yeah.
One day Goth already was checking on what he was doing, and he didn't like something.
He called him a slap in the face, or the face got swollen.
Either way.
Goth, Chilowitch He didn't know him yet.
And next day, or two days later, he came over there.
He says, what happened to your face?
Oh, nothing.
Nothing.
He didn't tell him he hit him.
No, nothing.
I got bitten by a bee.
And Goth liked this and made him big shot over there, you know?
And that's why he became chief of the concentration camp.
A powerful experience is when one day they cleaned the ghetto and I lost my parents.
I was in the ghetto.
My wife and my parents were staying over there because you have to line over there.
And the German came, and this on this side, and this on this side.
And there was a few seconds, and I never saw my parents since then.
They came in the morning.
During the night, they surrounded the ghetto with army, police, everything, and called in the morning everybody out.
Form a line here, and there will start separating you now.
So that's the way-- that was the way it works in ghetto.
I lost my parents in two seconds.
In the same month, I got a-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] my brother was killed in Mielec.
Over there they shot him.
And one month I lost the whole family.
I stayed.
Only my wife survived.
No, this was-- this was another thing, and I got Madritsch was protecting us.
Madritsch-- we got-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] how you call this?
Certificates where we're working for Madritsch and they didn't touch us.
He came up with it, too, you know, and did what he could.
Then Goth needed this over the Madritsch in Plaszow, where they're producing for the army uniforms, this.
He needed this here.
I got no stuff already.
We got no more stuff.
We came to Krakow-Plaszow.
So we live in the barracks over there on a bunk, you know?
And whatever we could, you know?
We got some [INAUDIBLE] coats or we made slacks, pants, shoes, something, you know?
And we got a separate barracks with Madritsch because we went to work, so we have to appear we all went together, which was [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] about 15 minutes from the living quarters.
It was a small whole section, industry section, where you got all kind.
They made the paper barracks.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I mean, almost everything.
Separately.
Not only separately.
They were separated by barbed wire.
Yeah, through the wires.
Yeah, through the barbed wires.
Sure, we saw each other because she works in the same place I work.
So over there we were in the [INAUDIBLE] together.
End of the day, she went her way, I went my way.
Any crazy guy would get married in Plaszow.
He must be crazy.
I didn't see it.
I know he got parties over there, because he called all day from Gestapo, [? dig, ?] you know, together even.
And he invited Madritsch and Titsch, you know, because they gave me whisky, and cognac, and all kinds of thing.
But I know there were two guys that were there who used to play from the camp.
The name is Rosner.
Two brothers.
One is still alive, and his wife and his brother I think is in Australia.
They used to call them.
He used to play-- he used to a whole band, orchestra, before the war.
So I used to go over there, [INAUDIBLE] playing for them.
But I don't know what's all inside over there.
We weren't interested.
We got enough trouble here.
Conditions-- like I told you, because of Madritsch we got little much-- because in the morning when we came nobody was staying over your head.
We went with water.
There was latrines-- you know, one over there, over there.
Shower self, shave by yourself.
Went back to the barracks over there, and got some food.
And then we work.
But then you got the-- once a week I think we had to go through lousing.
You know what lousing is?
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] It's something-- they would put steam on you so you shouldn't have lice, because the Germans were afraid.
If we get catch of the lice and typhus, they're going to get it too in the camp.
So they took care of this.
And then, no.
We got-- in Madritsch we didn't obsess because different work over there.
Because no German could get in over there, so we got little-- except, Goth.
He came.
He didn't answer to no one.
No.
No.
He just asked a question or something, and no.
No.
Barracks were about 200 people in the barracks.
Day and night it was noise because people come and go.
Night shift [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] came in the morning, so we went over there.
So you live with noise all the time, but you were so tired that you fell asleep.
That's it.
Sure.
On the one bunk-- one bunk was how many in the width?
About four people separate.
And there were no closets for anything.
You slept in the same clothing when you worked over there.
No pajamas.
Roll call every day and every evening.
We went to work in the morning, which was usually six o'clock, winter or summer.
And after we came back at six o'clock in the evening the same with the roll calls.
Like I said, sometimes when somebody died during the night, you have to bring the dead body to count it.
If no, there were problems.
Oh, sure.
You wake up in the morning, you got a  dead body near you.
Say hello to him.
You got used to it.
It didn't bother you.
Didn't bother you anymore.
Took him out, and that's it.
After a while, you know, like the surgeon, he studies over there, like surgery.
The first two days, he vomits, and then he gets used it.
He eat a sandwich and perform surgery.
If anybody tried, never spoke about it to nobody.
Nobody trusted another guy.

And I know a few guys run away, and we paid for this, because every time when somebody run away, when he run away, when you work outside Goth camp and came back was missing, they took five guys and shot on the spot in front of everybody because they run away.
I remember Goth said, it should be a lesson to you.
You're responsible for the guy.
When you went to the latrine, you couldn't go by yourself.
You have to have five guys with you, because latrine-- it was about a half mile from the place where you work.
So five guys-- you have to look for four more guys to go with you to the latrine, because everybody was responsible for another one.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] There always was a [INAUDIBLE] over there.
Hanging.
By hanging, by shooting.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] There was a place over there which they called this in Polish-- it's not a nice word-- Hujowa Gorka.
Hujowa Gorka.
They mention the book too.
There was a hill and around the hill was like graves.
And they put over [INAUDIBLE] and shot them with machine guns.
And later they came with gasoline, put fire, burn them over there.
It was daily, because they brought from outside people, too, which they find outside somebody, and they brought him over there, and this was everyday.
Frequent hanging, frequent shooting.
You got the-- you came to camp, and then, oh, he got today shot.
This guy got hanged, this, and this, and that's-- so everybody feeling as long as it's not me, [INAUDIBLE].
As much-- because, sure, you want-- people came over there and sold you cigarettes, which they got from outside.
You gave him bread.
It was business, you know, all day.
Since 19-- through 1944, until November when we went to Brunnlitz.
Everything loose over there, you know.
They didn't watch so much.
The roll calls-- because Goth was gone, and everybody felt that something is going-- the war is to end.
You know what I mean?
So the bad guys tried to be nice all of a sudden, because if they survive and the is over, they're going to pay for the things--
They took him out after they find out he killed his six, seven people without Berlin's order.
You know what I mean?
There was all the [INAUDIBLE].
We came out in 1943 or 1944.
You cannot punish or shot somebody without a written order from Berlin.
Actually, when you present your case and make the decision you can do it or not.
And he did it over there without the written [INAUDIBLE].
They took away all his medals, uniforms.
Out.
He lost then two weeks after this maybe 50 pounds, because he came once to visit us in Brunnlitz.
Couldn't recognize him.
He was a dope addict, too.
Not details.
No.
No, no.
We [INAUDIBLE] praying to be bombed.
It was situation, you know, that you got enough already.
We couldn't, because you work all the time.
Sometimes there were guys who kept a count over there.
They went to the corner over there and prayed, but not openly, because--
But on Yom Kippur, they shot most people, any people, the Germans.
Know what I mean?
All right.
Sure.
There were a few people over there that kept-- I remember Passover.
I remember one guy with a piece of bread he go that he couldn't eat he exchanged for potatoes.
Well, listen, maybe they had with them calendars, I don't know.
But a few of them, sure.
I know.
Sure.
No day off.
I think Sunday we work a half day only to give us a chance to wash your clothes, anything.
And sometimes they call me, Henry, come over, he says, because we haven't gotten enough [INAUDIBLE] coats cut for the night shift.
I went with the cutters and we stayed over there another six, seven hours, because no work, no life.
We didn't know what they were in the last day of camp.
We didn't know.
We knew only one thing-- the camp would be liquidated.
Some people would be transferred to different camps.
We-- through Madritsch I, and my wife, and a few others knew are we going with Schindler, because he paid them all.
Got some agreement with them.
Now, you want to know why Madritsch didn't open the camp--
Let me give you a story.
Schindler was born in Czechoslovakia, was a Nazi, worked for SA-- the brown uniforms.
And he was a fifth column in Czechoslovakia.
So when Germany occupied Czechoslovakia, not Schindler.
He was a smart, shrewd guy.
I mean, they can't take from him.
They tried to reward him for this.
So send him to Krakow.
He took over pots and pan factory.
They called it [INAUDIBLE].
And the owners used to still be over there.
They got rid of [INAUDIBLE].
They taught him the tricks.
And he start making for the army and on the side sell on black market.
He made a lot of money.
So much money he didn't know what to do with the money.
He brought jewelry, and this, and gifts.
So finally 1942, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] he realized-- he got the connection.
The war is-- they're losing the war in German.
He was smart enough to figure something out, what to do so I can save myself and the money I made.
So the only thing alternative was the Jews.
He was going to save Jews.
He was going to protect them, so end of the war they can tell him that he helped us.
He save us.
Now, the question is, why Madritsch didn't?
Madritsch was a regular businessman in Vienna.
By the way, Goth is too from Vienna-- Austria.
Well, he got this factory, he did whatever he could.
But he didn't have this problem that he was a Nazi, or he did something, he killed people so after the war, they're going to look for him.
And the war-- when they liquidated the thing, he says, listen, I can do so much for your people, and I tried to save some people with Schindler, but that's the only thing I can do.
He didn't have such a connection like Schindler, and he wasn't maybe so shrewd like Schindler.
And second, he didn't have to do it.
I mean, nobody will tell me how Schindler was a Jew lover.
Who?
Madritsch?
Went back to Vienna.
He opened a business over there.
He lived a nice few years over there, and a lot of people who visit him.
He and Titsch.
And I remember Titsch used to hide money for some people over there.
He came-- after the war, they went to him.
He gave them every penny.
Right.
We were put on a transport.
Very nervous because-- sure, because we figured out, listen, who can trust the Germans?
So we went.
First of all, they brought us through.
They separated us from women.
There 700 men and 300 women, mostly wives, daughters, you know, families.
So [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] all of a sudden we find ourselves in a transport.
You went to [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] Gross Rosen, which was a terrible camp.
All the men.
